description: Pediatric fever in a 2-year-old; high temp but otherwise well-appearing. Defensible ESI 2 or 3.

patient.name: Liam P., 2M
patient.chief_complaint: Fever to 39.5°C for 24 hours, irritable but consolable, decreased oral intake but tolerating sips, no rash, no respiratory distress, brief tactile fever overnight. Parent reports two wet diapers in last 8 hours.
patient.history: Healthy 2-year-old, immunizations up to date, no recent illness

rubric_notes: Pediatric fever > 39 in toddler = elevated risk floor (occult bacteremia, UTI, otitis, viral). HR 138 is age-appropriate tachycardia for fever (compensatory) and not by itself critical for a 2-year-old. RR 28 elevated but mild for fever. Defensible ESI 2 (high fever in young child = prompt evaluation including urine + ?LP if ill-appearing) or ESI 3 (stable, well-appearing, single resource workup with urine analysis + symptomatic management). Many EDs use ESI 3 for well-appearing febrile toddlers; ESI 2 if ill-appearing. The rubric's adult-default vitals trip critical-tier here (temp >39.5) but pediatric context softens this — admit author judgment via acceptable_esi.
